139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**

207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.

\begin{lemma}[Scalar tower on a bundled restriction-of-scalars module]
  \label{lem:isScalarTower_restrictScalars_obj}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isScalarTower_restrictScalars_obj}
  Let \(R \to S\) be a ring map and let \(M\) be an \(S\)-module. Then the \(R\)-module obtained from
  \(M\) by restriction of scalars along \(R \to S\), written
  \((\mathrm{restrictScalars}_{R \to S})\,M\), carries a compatible scalar-tower structure
  \(\mathrm{IsScalarTower}\,R\,S\): the restricted \(R\)-action factors through the \(S\)-action via the
  algebra map \(R \to S\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  By definition of restriction of scalars the \(R\)-action on \(M\) is
  \(r \cdot m = (\text{algebraMap}\,r) \cdot m\); this is exactly the hypothesis of the scalar-tower
  criterion that builds \(\mathrm{IsScalarTower}\,R\,S\) from an algebra map whose induced action agrees
  with the ambient \(S\)-action. Hence \(R\), \(S\) and the algebra map satisfy the scalar-tower identity
  on \((\mathrm{restrictScalars}_{R \to S})\,M\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Reconciliation equivalence for the tile section comparison]
  \label{lem:tileReconcileEquiv}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tileReconcileEquiv, AlgebraicGeometry.tileReconcileEquiv_apply,
    AlgebraicGeometry.tileReconcileEquiv_symm_apply}
  \uses{lem:tile_scalar_compat_genV, lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq}
  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}\)-module, let \(g \in R\), write \(R_g\)
  for the localisation of \(R\) away from \(g\), and let \(V \subseteq \Spec R_g\) be an open. Let
  \(\iota : \Spec R_g \xrightarrow{\sim} D(g) \hookrightarrow \Spec R\) be the localisation open
  immersion, so that \(\iota(V)\) is the iterated image of \(V\) under the affine identification
  \(\Spec R_g \cong D(g)\) followed by the open inclusion \(D(g) \hookrightarrow \Spec R\). Then there is
  an \(R\)-linear isomorphism
  \[
    (\mathrm{restrictScalars}_{R \to R_g})\, \Gamma_{R_g}\bigl(V, \mathcal{F}_{(g)}\bigr)
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
      \Gamma_R\bigl(\iota(V),\ \mathcal{F}\bigr),
  \]
  which is the identity on underlying elements. The forward and inverse maps are the identity on
  elements (the two apply identities), so the only content is the reconciliation of the two module
  structures carried by the common section type.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:tile_scalar_compat_genV, lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq}
  The two section modules share one underlying carrier: on underlying elements the tile section over
  \(V\) is the section of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the image open \(\iota(V)\), by the
  \(\mathrm{modulesSpecToSheaf} \circ \mathrm{restrict}\) carrier identity (a definitional equality). The
  underlying map is therefore the identity, and it is additive on the nose. Its \(R\)-linearity is
  exactly the general-open scalar-tower compatibility of Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_scalar_compat_genV}: the
  native \(R\)-action on the \(\mathcal{F}\)-side (described by
  Lemma~\ref{lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq}) coincides with the \(R_g\)-action read through the
  localisation map \(R \to R_g\), i.e.\ the restriction-of-scalars action carried by the tile side. The
  identity map is thus \(R\)-linear with \(R\)-linear inverse, hence an \(R\)-linear isomorphism.
\end{proof}
